items.rationale: Sees a wide-open theoretical frontier: characterizing/optimizing trading functions (Uniswap, Curve, Balancer), formalizing LP economics, and importing the selfish-routing/price-of-anarchy toolbox into DeFi. Repeatedly returns to it as fertile, under-theorized ground.
items.age_note: Optimism first stated Nov 2021; reinforced by sustained LVR/AMM work through Nov 2022, so current not stale.

citations.quote: we don't have a theory of DeFi just yet, but I'm very optimistic that we'll see a lot of interesting work over the next couple of years
this connection unlocks the whole selfish routing/price of anarchy toolbox for the analysis of trading in a network of CFMMs. Very cool!

items.rationale: Views succinct proofs as 'magic' computational compression tied to the PCP theorem, now becoming practical; actively tracking prover-time bottleneck progress and hired Justin Thaler. Sees this as the core of web3 scaling infrastructure.

citations.quote: Excited to watch how all the SNARK-related storylines are going to play out--the suspense is killing me!
The fact that you can compress arbitrary computations in this way is shocking, IMO.

items.rationale: His central research program: building a general theory of permissionless consensus he believes could be 'as rich as' the permissioned theory. Calls 2022 DAG-based consensus advances among the year's most exciting developments.

citations.quote: One of the most exciting developments in consensus protocols in 2022 has been the recent advances in DAG-based consensus.
there's a general theory of permissionless consensus ... waiting to be developed, plausibly as rich as the existing theory for the permissioned setting

items.rationale: Frames cryptoeconomics as mechanism design with a native currency (mint/burn power) and lays out 'grand challenges': optimal L1 incentives and bringing macroeconomics rigor to supply-cap debates. Sees transaction-fee mechanisms (EIP-1559) as a model success.

citations.quote: Grand challenge #2: Optimal layer-1 incentives. (Holy grail: matching possibility and impossibility results as complete as in the theory of consensus protocols.)
the old first-price transaction fee mechanism now replaced by a congestion-dependent posted-price-style mechanism that has superior incentive guarantees

items.rationale: Frames blockchains not as money or databases but as a shared programmable 'computer that lives in the sky'; left academia to lead a16z crypto research because he sees a multidisciplinary field blossoming with ground-floor opportunity.

citations.quote: a new computing paradigm—–a programmable computer that lives in the sky, that is not owned by anyone and that anyone can use
witnessing a new multidisciplinary field (spurred by web3) blossom before our eyes. There are enormous opportunities to shape this field

items.rationale: Hosted seminars and co-authored work on near-term improvements to on-chain voting schemes, treating web3 governance as a serious cross-disciplinary problem (learning from traditional political science).

citations.quote: immediately-actionable local improvements to existing voting schemes ... are complementary to the more ambitious non-transferable governance program

items.rationale: Daily conversations even with smart people reveal deep confusion conflating tech, behavior, and investment. Sees clickbait framings (e.g. 'it's gambling') as off-base and an urgent obstacle to clear thinking about the technology.

citations.quote: Every day I have conversations---generally with very smart people---that remind me just how much confusion there currently is around web3
there's a lot of urgent work to do educating people to think clearly about web3

items.rationale: Argues 'impermanent loss' is a misleading term that understates the real, often permanent adverse selection LPs suffer to arbitrageurs; pushes LVR as the proper loss measure. A structural design problem AMMs must solve.
items.age_note: Concern raised Oct 2021; sharpened and formalized via LVR work through Sept–Nov 2022 — current.

citations.quote: the adverse selection suffered by liquidity providers in an automated market maker, which typically leads to some permanent loss
The point of our paper is that IL is not the best loss measure.

items.rationale: Flags as an open grand challenge whether MEV is fundamental to any decentralized smart-contract platform or merely an artifact of current designs — an unresolved risk to incentive soundness across the stack.

citations.quote: is MEV fundamental to any decentralized smart contract platform or an artifact of current designs?

items.rationale: Recent results show no PoW protocol can have both synchronous liveness and partially-synchronous consistency (honest nodes can't tell waning hashrate from delayed messages); escaping it needs hashrate-stability assumptions he deems indefensible in PoW.

citations.quote: No proof-of-work protocol satisfies both liveness in the synchronous model and consistency in the partially synchronous model
Not clear that such assumptions are defensible in a PoW setting.

items.rationale: Repeatedly objects to terms that mislead newcomers/students — 'zk rollups' (no zero knowledge), 'impermanent loss', 'blockchain as database' — seeing imprecise vocabulary as a barrier to a sound theory.

citations.quote: validity rollups (often misleadingly called zk rollups, despite the lack of zero knowledge)
I have no idea why the "blockchain as DB" meme has persisted for as long as it has.

items.rationale: Cites EIP-1559 as a mechanism-design success with superior incentive guarantees and long-run deflationary consequences; treats Ethereum as the canonical programmable-computer platform and the substrate for validity rollups. Excitement is about the protocol/mechanism, not price.

citations.quote: the EIP-1559 transaction fee mechanism ... macroeconomic consequences (ETH likely deflationary in the long run)
Big day for Ethereum ... a congestion-dependent posted-price-style mechanism that has superior incentive guarantees

items.rationale: Technical, not price-based concern: as PoW longest-chain it loses consistency under network partition and offers weaker guarantees than BFT; and its hard supply cap means block rewards →0, raising undercutting/re-org attack worries. Analytical caution about the design, not a bearish trade.

citations.quote: Macroeconomic policy decision (hard supply cap) => microeconomic consequences (e.g., undercutting re-org attacks as the block reward goes to 0)
Longest-chain consensus loses consistency under network attacks (in the partially synchronous model)

risk_regime.rationale: Roughgarden never expresses a market/price risk posture — no commentary on prices, cycles, allocation, or the late-2022 market downturn/FTX. His writings are research-driven and structurally optimistic about the long-term technology and field ('enormous opportunities to shape this field'), while methodologically cautious: he prizes impossibility results, flags open problems, and once returned/burned a token allocation after being misled. Net: long-horizon structural optimism on the technology paired with rigorous skepticism about specific designs — but effectively no view on market risk-on/risk-off.

notes: Tim Roughgarden's corpus is that of an academic researcher and a16z crypto Head of Research, not a markets/trading voice — so 'excited/concerned' map to research frontiers and design soundness, not token bets. Highest-conviction, most-recent themes (late 2022): consensus theory (incl. DAG-based), SNARKs/validity rollups, LVR/AMM design, and cryptoeconomic incentive design. Token views are deliberately conservative: ETH excitement is about EIP-1559 mechanism design and the rollup-centric roadmap; BTC concern is a technical critique of PoW guarantees, not a price call. He is consistent over the 18-month window with no major reversals; the term-preference and 'blockchain-is-a-computer' stances persisted and were restated. No statements reference the FTX collapse or 2022 bear market, so any market-risk inference is extrapolated.
